issues.description: resumePipeline fetches the run twice: once in PipelineApiImpl for the authorization check, and once inside PipelineService.resumeRun(). Consider passing the already-fetched run to the service or extracting the auth check into a shared utility to avoid the redundant DB query.

SubscriptionAuthorizationException message includes the subscriptionId ('User is not authorized to access subscription <UUID>'). For getPipelineRun and resumePipeline, the caller only provides a runId, so the 403 response body leaks the internal subscription UUID (users.id) to an unauthorized user. Consider using a generic message without the subscription ID, or at least omit it in the HTTP response (the PipelineErrorHandler currently passes ex.message directly).

The verifySubscriptionOwnership private method lives in the controller (PipelineApiImpl). If other controllers or services need the same check in the future, consider extracting it into a reusable authorization service. For now this is fine given the single-controller scope.

sharedStateObservations.description: AGENTS.md lists pipeline domain port interfaces as off-limits but the new SubscriptionOwnershipPort was correctly added as a NEW port (not a modification). Consider updating AGENTS.md to either add SubscriptionOwnershipPort to the off-limits list or clarify the rule applies only to modifying existing ports, not creating new ones.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: AGENTS.md says 'Domain port interfaces: LlmPort.kt, SearchPort.kt, ObservabilityPort.kt, StepResultPort.kt, PipelineRunPort.kt, NotificationPort.kt — do NOT modify'. New file SubscriptionOwnershipPort.kt was created at stayposted-backend/src/main/kotlin/io/stayposted/pipeline/domain/port/SubscriptionOwnershipPort.kt

sharedStateObservations.description: AGENTS.md review-fixes context notes that 'subscriptionId == users.id' is the ownership model (the SubscriptionOwnershipAdapter queries users.id = subscriptionId). This domain mapping is non-obvious and could be documented in AGENTS.md or library for future workers, since the feature description had to tell the worker to 'investigate the codebase' to discover it.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: SubscriptionOwnershipAdapter.kt queries Tables.USERS.ID.eq(subscriptionId) and Tables.USERS.SUPABASE_USER_ID.eq(userId.toString()). The feature description said 'IMPORTANT: Before implementing, investigate the codebase to understand how subscriptions relate to users.'

sharedStateObservations.description: Worker followed the pipeline-worker skill procedure (read context, implement, verify). The skill suggests 'Write Tests First (Red)' but the worker implemented code and tests together. This is acceptable for authorization cross-cutting concerns but the deviation wasn't flagged.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Handoff skillFeedback.followedProcedure=true, but the commit includes both production code and tests in a single commit (no red phase evidence). This is pragmatic for a review-fix but differs from the skill's TDD mandate.

summary: The implementation correctly adds subscription-level authorization to all three pipeline API endpoints (triggerPipeline, getPipelineRun, resumePipeline). The architectural approach is clean: a SubscriptionOwnershipPort domain interface with a jOOQ adapter, a dedicated exception type with a 403 error handler, and consistent use of AuthenticationContext.get() in each endpoint. All three VAL-AUTH validation items are covered by explicit integration tests. The authorization cannot be bypassed — it runs after authentication and before any business logic. Two non-blocking issues found: a double DB fetch for resumePipeline and a minor information leak of subscription UUID in error responses. Overall, this is a solid implementation that addresses the review finding M2 completely.
